It receives a task and carries it out on its own, step by step, until the job is done. In this article, we explain what AI agents actually are, where they can already be used today and how the major platforms on the market differ from one another. ### Privacy and data security First, it is important to understand that agents work differently from regular AI tools. They can access accounts, browse websites and sometimes retain information between conversations. Before connecting an agent to your email, calendar or sensitive data, read the privacy policy of the specific tool and ask: Are my data used for training? Does the agent save history? These are the kinds of questions worth considering before beginning to work with AI agents. ### What is an AI agent? The word “agent” means someone who acts on behalf of someone else. In the world of artificial intelligence, the term “AI with agency” is used to describe AI that can act independently to achieve a goal, adjust its strategy while working and use memory and context to complete complex tasks. In practice, an AI agent is a system that can take a task and perform it independently, step by step, without requiring you to tell it what to do at every stage. It does not stop after each step and ask, “What now?” You can think of an AI agent as an AI model that has also been given tools and the ability to act independently: to browse the internet, use applications, make decisions and complete complex tasks. highlights.content: [Skip to content](#main-content) [LDS Let's Data ScienceLEARN • BUILD • STAY AHEAD](/) [Sign in](/login?redirect=%2Fnews%2Famazon-explores-selling-trainium-chips-to-data-centres-b86b4fe9) Infrastructureamazontrainiumai chipsnvidia competition # Amazon explores selling Trainium chips to data centres | 7.0 Relevance Score Amazon is exploring selling its custom **Trainium** AI chips to third-party data centres, according to reporting by Bloomberg and TechCrunch. AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg that AWS is in talks to allow other organisations to use Trainium outside of AWS. The conversations are described in coverage as early-stage, per TechCrunch. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy wrote in his April shareholder letter that, if the chip business operated standalone and sold externally, it could reach an annual run rate of around **$50 billion**, a figure cited by TechCrunch and India Today. Reporting frames the move as a more direct challenge to **Nvidia** for AI infrastructure capacity, while sources note existing production and allocation constraints for Trainium capacity. ### What happened Amazon is exploring external sales of its custom AI chip family, **Trainium**, for use in other companies' data centres, according to reporting by Bloomberg and TechCrunch. AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg that AWS is in talks to allow third parties to use Trainium in their own data centres, per TechCrunch and Bloomberg. TechCrunch and India Today report the discussions are at an early stage. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy wrote in his April shareholder letter that a standalone chips business selling to AWS and external customers could reach an annual run rate of around **$50 billion**, a figure cited in public coverage by TechCrunch and India Today. Public reporting also frames the proposal as a potential competitive move against **Nvidia**, which currently dominates AI accelerator sales, per TechCrunch and Bloomberg. ### Editorial analysis - technical context Companies selling custom AI accelerators outside their own clouds introduces both supply-chain and integration challenges. Industry-pattern observations: vendors that supply chips to third-party data centres typically need to secure high-volume manufacturing capacity from foundry partners such as **TSMC**, handle firmware/driver support, and provide system-level integration guidance for rack-level deployments. Industry-pattern observations: cloud providers that internalise accelerators and then externalise sales must balance allocation between retail cloud customers and external purchasers, which can create short-term capacity constraints and lead times for compute-hungry customers. ### Context and significance If realised at scale, third-party sales of cloud-designed accelerators reshape where hyperscalers exert influence over the server and rack market and increase vendor choices for organisations building localized AI infrastructure. Reporting frames Amazon's discussions as a direct competitive pressure on **Nvidia**, but sources indicate the effort is nascent and constrained by current production and allocation dynamics. ### Industry context Andy Jassy's **$50 billion** run-rate figure, reported in his shareholder letter and cited by multiple outlets, signals the revenue potential Amazon attributes to hardware sales if the business model changes; that figure is a public claim in Amazon's own shareholder communication, not an independent forecast. ### What to watch * •Production and supply: observers should monitor announcements or filings that name foundry partners or capacity expansion deals, which would indicate the feasibility of large-scale external sales. * •Sales model and packaging: whether Amazon offers standalone chips, full servers, or rack systems will affect total cost of ownership for buyers and competitive positioning versus vendors that sell both accelerators and integrated systems. * •Third-party uptake and contracts: named customer deals or pilot deployments repor...

highlights.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Research Professional News](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com) [Published by Clarivate](https://clarivate.com/) [Go back](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/category/uk/uk-universities/) [Universities](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/category/uk/uk-universities/) # Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids By Sophie Hogan Share * [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) * [Share on X](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/&text=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids) * [Share on BlueSky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids%20https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) * [Share on Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/&title=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids&summary=&source=) * [Share via email](mailto:?body=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) *Image source: [ImageryBT](https://stock.adobe.com/uk/images/aerial-view-of-harrogate-convention-centre-and-town-in-north-yorkshire-uk/543688586), via Adobe Stock* **Arma 2026: Stance on generative AI softens, but final funding decisions still taken “by humans”** Research funders in the UK are clarifying their position on generative artificial intelligence to allow its use in the processing of grant applications, members of a group of major funders have said. At the Association for Research Managers and Administrators (Arma) conference on 18 June, representatives from UK Research and Innovation and Wellcome said the Research Funding Policy Group—which includes UKRI and Wellcome alongside six other major funders—will soon update their joint position on the use of generative AI tools in funding applications and assessment. The group released a statement in 2023 which said the use of generative AI “presents potential risks for research”, while individual funders have gone further in their own policies. Wellcome’s policy says “we do not use generative AI tools to assess the quality of grant applications or to aid funding decisions”, while UKRI’s policy says that assessors, including reviewers and panelists, “must not use generative AI tools as part of their assessment activities”. **Shift in stance** But speaking at Arma, Lydia Fulford, senior funding policy manager at UKRI, said the joint statement would be updated such that funders “may use generative AI to process funding applications” but that, “as ever, we will not put a whole application into a publicly available generative AI tool”. Fulford also said the clarified statement will “include that all final funding decisions will be undertaken by humans”. The shift in position by the Research Funding Policy Group—which also includes the Association of Medical Research Charities, the British Heart Foundation, Cancer Research UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Research, the Royal Academy of Engineering and the Royal Society—comes as funders [grapple with skyrocketing volumes](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-4-grant-systems-could-collapse-with-rise-in-ai-driven-demand/) of grant applications. There are suggestions that to tackle such growth in applications, funders could turn to AI, and UKRI has funded a project led by the University of...

highlights.title: Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation - Telecoms
highlights.titleZh: Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation - Telecoms
highlights.content: * [AI](/digital-ecosystem/ai) * [Digital Ecosystem](/digital-ecosystem) * [News](/latest-news) # Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation Mavenir and Red Hat have unveiled a new platform that essentially enables direct carrier billing for AI services. [Nick Wood,](/author/nick-wood) Freelance writer June 19, 2026 3 Min Read They call it an Integrated AI Platform, and it brings together network software maker Mavenir's AI software platform and open source specialist Red Hat's enterprise Kubernetes and AI capabilities to provide a carrier-grade, on-premises, cloud native AI infrastructure platform. Once up and running, Mavenir says operators can monetise AI similarly to how they monetise data – by creating token-based consumption plans with full control over pricing and service level agreements (SLAs), with the fees added as an item on the customer's phone bill. Mavenir and Red Hat say their platform supports three operating models. It can be used as an enterprise AI platform where the operator offers metered access to AI models, compute resources and AI tooling to customers as a value-added service alongside core connectivity services. It can also function as AI grid infrastructure, where operators use the platform to charge customers for hosting AI applications and third-party workloads. Related: [Nokia plots 10-fold capacity increase at US chip facility](/ai/nokia-plots-10-fold-capacity-increase-at-us-chip-facility) Finally, operators can use the platform to offer their own AI products and services to their subscribers, including AI assistant services, billed on a token-consumption basis. The platform has been designed with on-premises AI models and small language models in mind; however, it also comes with the option of facilitating policy-governed access to external frontier models when enterprises are in need of a bit more oomph. "Operators are watching AI revenues flow to hyperscalers and third-party platforms while they provide the connectivity that makes it all possible. This changes today. The Integrated AI Platform developed in collaboration between Mavenir and Red Hat gives operators the infrastructure to become AI service providers in their own right," said Mavenir's chief technology and strategy officer, Bejoy Pankajakshan. "Operators gain sovereign control over models and data, token-accurate monetisation that integrates with their existing BSS, and the service assurance to back their own managed AI services with contractual SLAs. Whether operators are launching subscriber AI services, building out their AI grid infrastructure, or offering AI capabilities to enterprise customers on a consumption basis, the platform provides the commercial and operational controls to do it at carrier scale." Chris Wright, CTO and SVP of global engineering at Red Hat, chimed in with: "Working with Mavenir, we're delivering an integrated solution supported on the Kubernetes-native foundation powered by Red Hat AI, which brings MLOps, vLLM inference and AgentOps capabilities. Related: [AI app disruption is on the up](/ai/ai-app-disruption-is-on-the-up) "This hybrid architecture allows operators to run sovereign on-premises models for the bulk of their traffic and selectively connect to frontier models for tasks that require it, all through a single policy and billing framework." Keeping close tabs on access to external frontier models is likely to strike a chord with potential customers. The recent shift by the AI industry from flat-rate to consumption-based pricing models has echoes of the bill shocks experienced by end users in the early, heady days of metered mobile data usage. High-profile examples are in ample supply. The most oft-referenced one is Uber CTO Neppalli Naga telling  [The Information](https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/2ahACXD7MEUG9w4rkI6fncW0o6U?domain=theinformation.com) [(paywall)](https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/2ahACXD7MEUG9w4rkI6fncW0o6U?domain=theinformation.com)  that his company had...

Variety quotes Minyoung Kim, Netflix VP of content for APAC (ex-India): "I always consider myself and my team as portfolio managers." Variety reports MD Entertainment founder Manoj Punjabi said global streaming services are important gateways for Indonesian stories. Variety reports Media Partners Asia CEO Vivek Couto framed **AI** as having moved from conference talking point to operational reality; JioHotstar chief architect Vijay Seshadri told Variety the platform deployed a conversational voice discovery feature built with OpenAI, with more than **60%** of users choosing voice over text. Panels at APOS covered GenAI, AI-native filmmaking, AI-powered localization and AI video generation, Variety reports. ### What happened Variety reports that the APOS conference in Bali presented eight themes setting the agenda for Asia's entertainment sector in 2026, with an emphasis on **IP**, sport, vertical content and government incentives. Variety quotes Minyoung Kim, Netflix VP of content for APAC (ex-India): "I always consider myself and my team as portfolio managers," while Variety reports MD Entertainment founder Manoj Punjabi described global streaming services as gateways for Indonesian stories. Variety reports Media Partners Asia CEO Vivek Couto opened the summit by framing **AI** as moved from a talking point to operational reality. Variety reports JioHotstar chief architect Vijay Seshadri said the platform has deployed a conversational voice discovery feature built with OpenAI, and that more than **60%** of users now choose voice over text for discovery. Variety reports panels included discussions on GenAI, AI-native filmmaking, AI-powered localization and AI video generation. ### Editorial analysis - technical context Editorial analysis: The shift described at APOS mirrors a common industry progression where generative AI moves from pilot projects into integrated product features such as conversational search, localization pipelines and assistive production tools. Platforms that integrate voice-first discovery and AI-assisted localization typically face engineering workstreams around latency, model governance and dataset localization, and those are the operational challenges practitioners should expect when similar features scale. ### Context and significance For the Asia-Pacific market, the APOS narrative highlights two concurrent dynamics: rising emphasis on **IP** as exportable content across formats, and the embedding of AI across the content value chain. The reported JioHotstar deployment is a concrete example of a streaming platform applying OpenAI-based components to user-facing discovery, which illustrates broader adoption patterns across content platforms rather than a single-vendor technical breakthrough. ### What to watch For practitioners: Track adoption signals such as user engagement metrics for voice discovery (reported **60%** usage at JioHotstar), production workflows adopting AI-native tools, and the emergence of tooling for AI-powered localization and rights-aware asset management. Observers should also watch how publishers and producers operationalize IP as cross-format franchises alongside AI-enabled production and distribution workflows. ## Scoring Rationale The story documents concrete AI deployment signals in APAC streaming and industry-level shifts toward IP-centric franchises. highlights.titleZh: Snap Spins Off Its Gen-AI Video Team Into New Company 06/22/2026 - MediaPost
highlights.content: #### While other MediaPost newsletters and articles remain free to all ... our new Research Intelligencer service is reserved for paid subscribers ... Subscribe today to gain access to every *Research Intelligencer* article we publish as well as the exclusive daily newsletter, full access to *The MediaPost Cases*, first-look research and daily insights from Joe Mandese, Editor in Chief. [Become a subscriber today!](/subscribe/None/) # Become a free MediaPost member now to read this article * Unlimited articles every day * Keep up-to-date with media, marketing and advertising news * Invitations to exclusive industry events and research Subscribe to *MediaDailyNews* # Snap Spins Off Gen-AI Video Team To Form New Firm, Dotmo * by [Colin Kirkland](/publications/author/8358/colin-kirkland/) , 17 minutes ago In a move to diversify its AI business operations, Snap is spinning off its generative AI team into a new, independent entity called Dotmo that will focus on the development of artificial intelligence (AI) models and immersive gaming experiences. According to a report from TechCrunch, Dotmo will operate with a license that allows the company to adapt Snap’s proprietary technology for gaming and interactive entertainment platforms.   The new entity will also consist of Snap’s current generative-AI video team, which has decided to technically leave Snap to work on projects unrelated to the social-media platform.  Due to the rising costs of developing AI models and immersive gaming experiences internally, Snap has decided to connect its generative AI video team to a separate company called Dotmo. advertisement advertisement A representative from Snap told TechCrunch that while the planned digital experiences at Dotmo will exist separately from the social media company’s core business, future partnerships with Dotmo may be considered if the fit seems right. Snap will receive a large equity stake in Dotmo, while Dotmo may eventually seek funding outside of Bobby Murphy, Snap CTO and lead investor in Dotmo. Murphy will possess a “significant stake in the new firm,” the report reads. In addition, the report says, “through a financial backer, Murphy will continue to work for Snap full-time as its CTO and continue to lead its GenAI research and development initiatives.” Despite retaining 950 million daily active users while growing its revenue across all markets in Q1 2026, Snap has been struggling with establishing a clearer path to net-income profitability. The company’s stock price plummeted this week after its new generation of [Specs](https://www.mediapost.com/publications/article/415842/snap-unveils-new-gen-ar-glasses-plans-late-2026-l.html) was unveiled, adding another potential challenge in Snap’s long-term revenue plans.  Snap cited high costs in the development of AI models and immersive gaming experiences as a major reason for the creation of its Dotmo spinoff.  If Dotmo is successful, the entity could help cut Snap’s AI-investment costs, while also expanding its creative portfolio. highlights.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # AI and National Security: US Bans Export of Anthropic Models ·2·[Technology](/en/technology/) The US government has ordered [Anthropic](/en/tags/anthropic/) to restrict the export of its most powerful AI models — Fable and Mythos — due to national security concerns. This ban applies not only to foreign states but also to foreign citizens within the US. Following this unexpected decision, the AI giant completely removed both models from use, and they have been unavailable to anyone for a week. This is reported by Techcrunch.com [report](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzA2LzE5L2VuY3J5cHRpb24tc3B5d2FyZS1hbmQtbm93LW15dGhvcy1oaXN0b3J5LXNob3dzLXdoeS1jeWJlci1leHBvcnQtY29udHJvbC1kb2VzbnQtd29yay8%3D) says. This event is a unique test for the US government. Whether the Washington administration can curb advanced AI technologies through export control mechanisms remains to be seen. Previous similar attempts related to encryption technologies and spyware did not yield the expected results. According to ixbt.com, this conflict could set new rules that not only Anthropic but all other AI laboratories must follow. ## Why were Mythos and Fable deemed dangerous? When Anthropic introduced the Mythos model in April, it described it as a powerful tool that could cause significant devastation in cyberspace. Therefore, even before the ban, access to this model was granted only to 150 vetted companies and government organizations. The goal was to help defenders strengthen their software before malicious actors could gain such capabilities. It is said that two main events triggered the activation of the ban. First, Anthropic allowed South Korea's SK Telecom operator to use the Mythos model. US officials suspect the company's ties to China, although SK Telecom has denied these claims. Second, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy warned the government that a way to bypass the protection systems in the Fable 5 model had been found. While Anthropic called this error minor, the US Department of Commerce immediately issued an export control directive. ## Historical Experience: Does Export Control Work? Governments' attempts to limit the spread of cyber technologies are not new, but history shows few successes in this regard. In the 1990s, the US government viewed the PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) encryption software as a weapon and opened a criminal case against its creator, Phil Zimmermann. However, Zimmermann published the program code in book form, placing it under freedom of speech protection, which paved the way for the encryption technologies used today in messengers like Signal and WhatsApp. Additionally, in the early 2010s, after it became known that Western-made spyware was used against dissidents in the Middle East, the Wassenaar Arrangement was expanded internationally. This agreement aims to restrict the export of technologies that can be used for both civilian and military purposes. The situation with Anthropic is the next important step in showing how effective these methods are in the age of AI. highlights.titleZh: Figma CEO explains why creative people shouldn't worry about AI-generated design - Business Insider
highlights.content: [Subscribe](https://www.businessinsider.com/subscription) [Newsletters](https://www.businessinsider.com/subscription/newsletter) [Tech](/tech) # Figma CEO explains why creative people shouldn't worry about AI-generated design By [Jacob Shamsian](https://www.businessinsider.com/author/jacob-shamsian) You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email. [Read in app](https://insider-app.onelink.me/4cpG/?af_js_web=true&af_ss_ver=2_3_0&af_dp=insider%3A%2F%2Fbi%2Fpost%2Ffigma-ceo-dylan-field-creative-careers-ai-graphic-design-2026-6&af_force_deeplink=true&is_retargeting=true&deep_link_value=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.businessinsider.com%2Ffigma-ceo-dylan-field-creative-careers-ai-graphic-design-2026-6&pid=businessinsider&c=post_page_share_bar_v2_smart_4.13.23 "Download the app") Copy link [Email](mailto:?subject=Figma CEO explains why creative people shouldn't worry about AI-generated design&body=Figma%20CEO%20explains%20why%20creative%20people%20shouldn't%20worry%20about%20AI-generated%20design%0D%0A%0D%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.businessinsider.com%2Ffigma-ceo-dylan-field-creative-careers-ai-graphic-design-2026-6& "Email") Facebook WhatsApp X LinkedIn Bluesky Threads Impact Link Save Saved This story is available exclusively to Business Insider subscribers. [Become an Insider](/subscription) and start reading now. Have an account? . That AI slop can't hurt you, according to Figma's top executive. Figma CEO and cofounder Dylan Field said creative people — like the graphic designers who use his company's tools — should find the AI era "a great time to be creative." AI models, he said, are trained on the "distribution of data" and typically create designs that people recognize as "average." Humans, on the other hand, can make something that hasn't been seen before, Field told The New York Times' "Hard Fork" podcast. "If you're in distribution, and you're not actually pushing the bounds, I think that you're in a worse shape than if you're actually going and exploring the frontier of human knowledge, creativity, and what you can put out in the world," Field said. It receives a task and carries it out on its own, step by step, until the job is done. In this article, we explain what AI agents actually are, where they can already be used today and how the major platforms on the market differ from one another. ### Privacy and data security First, it is important to understand that agents work differently from regular AI tools. They can access accounts, browse websites and sometimes retain information between conversations. Before connecting an agent to your email, calendar or sensitive data, read the privacy policy of the specific tool and ask: Are my data used for training? Does the agent save history? These are the kinds of questions worth considering before beginning to work with AI agents. ### What is an AI agent? The word “agent” means someone who acts on behalf of someone else. In the world of artificial intelligence, the term “AI with agency” is used to describe AI that can act independently to achieve a goal, adjust its strategy while working and use memory and context to complete complex tasks. In practice, an AI agent is a system that can take a task and perform it independently, step by step, without requiring you to tell it what to do at every stage. AI Agents.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # AI and National Security: US Bans Export of Anthropic Models ·2·[Technology](/en/technology/) The US government has ordered [Anthropic](/en/tags/anthropic/) to restrict the export of its most powerful AI models — Fable and Mythos — due to national security concerns. This ban applies not only to foreign states but also to foreign citizens within the US. Following this unexpected decision, the AI giant completely removed both models from use, and they have been unavailable to anyone for a week. This is reported by Techcrunch.com [report](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzA2LzE5L2VuY3J5cHRpb24tc3B5d2FyZS1hbmQtbm93LW15dGhvcy1oaXN0b3J5LXNob3dzLXdoeS1jeWJlci1leHBvcnQtY29udHJvbC1kb2VzbnQtd29yay8%3D) says. This event is a unique test for the US government. Whether the Washington administration can curb advanced AI technologies through export control mechanisms remains to be seen. Previous similar attempts related to encryption technologies and spyware did not yield the expected results. According to ixbt.com, this conflict could set new rules that not only Anthropic but all other AI laboratories must follow. ## Why were Mythos and Fable deemed dangerous? When Anthropic introduced the Mythos model in April, it described it as a powerful tool that could cause significant devastation in cyberspace. Therefore, even before the ban, access to this model was granted only to 150 vetted companies and government organizations. The goal was to help defenders strengthen their software before malicious actors could gain such capabilities. It is said that two main events triggered the activation of the ban. First, Anthropic allowed South Korea's SK Telecom operator to use the Mythos model. US officials suspect the company's ties to China, although SK Telecom has denied these claims. Second, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy warned the government that a way to bypass the protection systems in the Fable 5 model had been found. While Anthropic called this error minor, the US Department of Commerce immediately issued an export control directive. ## Historical Experience: Does Export Control Work? Governments' attempts to limit the spread of cyber technologies are not new, but history shows few successes in this regard. In the 1990s, the US government viewed the PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) encryption software as a weapon and opened a criminal case against its creator, Phil Zimmermann. However, Zimmermann published the program code in book form, placing it under freedom of speech protection, which paved the way for the encryption technologies used today in messengers like Signal and WhatsApp. Additionally, in the early 2010s, after it became known that Western-made spyware was used against dissidents in the Middle East, the Wassenaar Arrangement was expanded internationally. This agreement aims to restrict the export of technologies that can be used for both civilian and military purposes. The situation with Anthropic is the next important step in showing how effective these methods are in the age of AI. AI Agents.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # US Government Bans Anthropic Models: National Security or Political Game? ·32·[Technology](/en/technology/) An unexpected turn has occurred in the world of AI: the US government has decided to restrict the use of [Anthropic](/en/tags/anthropic/)'s newest and most powerful models — the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 platforms. While this step was taken from a national security perspective, it is causing significant debate and unexpected consequences in the tech world. This was reported by Techcrunch.com [reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS92aWRlby9pcy10aGUtdXMtZ292ZXJubWVudHMtYW50aHJvcGljLWJhbi1hY2NpZGVudGFsbHktaGVscGluZy10aGUtYnJhbmQv) states. The ban was reportedly triggered by a vulnerability discovered by [Amazon](/en/tags/amazon/) researchers. According to reports, specialists found a way to bypass the guardrails of the Fable 5 model. This could lead to the risk of AI being used for malicious purposes. According to TechCrunch, cybersecurity experts have criticized this decision and issued an open letter. ## Doubts behind the ban and the security issue Anthropic representatives and a group of independent researchers emphasize that these vulnerabilities are not unique to the Fable 5 model. Such "jailbreak" methods currently exist in almost all major language models on the market. Therefore, experts find the government's strict measures specifically against Anthropic products suspicious. Political analysts view this situation as the latest manifestation of the complex relationship between Anthropic and the current Trump administration. TechCrunch journalists discussed on the Equity podcast that political pressure, rather than just security, may be behind this ban. ## Unexpected publicity and IPO prospects Interestingly, this ban could bring unexpected benefits to the Anthropic brand. Typically, technologies deemed "dangerous" and banned at the government level spark a specific interest among investors and users. This is being perceived as an indirect signal that the model is indeed extremely powerful and capable. As Anthropic is currently preparing for its initial public offering (IPO) process, this hype could positively impact the value of the company's shares. For developers and large corporations, the Anthropic platform remains one of the most attractive directions. This situation is also significant for Uzbek specialists and AI enthusiasts. Such global restrictions define how AI technologies will be regulated in the future. The banning of Anthropic models could open new opportunities for competitors like OpenAI and Google, or conversely, start an era of strict censorship for the entire industry. Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says By Isaac Barbosa Share * [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/) * [Share on X](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/&text=Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says) * [Share on BlueSky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says%20https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/) * [Share on Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/&title=Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says&summary=&source=) * [Share via email](mailto:?body=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/) *Image source: [Paper Trident](https://www.gettyimages.co.uk/detail/illustration/people-sitting-around-round-table-top-view-royalty-free-illustration/1944763144), via Getty Images* **Arma 2026: Dsit-UKRI metascience unit also plans survey of how and why researchers use AI** A radical peer review programme that uses grant applicants as reviewers is promising but not “the holy grail” for the research sector, a metascience adviser at the Department for Science, Information and Technology has said. He also revealed that a survey of artificial intelligence use across the research system is in the works. Unlike traditional peer review methods, which rely on external volunteers, distributed peer review (DPR) requires that grant applicants review others on the same funding call. Scores are designed to have no bearing on the ranking of the reviewer’s own application. The method has been examined by the UK Metascience Unit—a joint venture between Dsit and UK Research and Innovation—and widely praised. A UKRI trial last year found that DPR shortened the assessment time of applications by [up to 65 per cent](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2025-7-ukri-to-scale-up-radical-change-to-peer-review/) compared with the funder’s typical calls, leading to it being rolled out across some major funding calls across research councils. **Misplaced evangelism?** At a session of the Association of Research Managers and Administrators conference on 18 June, DPR was presented as a potential response to pressures facing the research sector, including [soaring grant application volumes](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-5-funders-scramble-to-limit-demand-as-research-grant-applications-rocket/). However, Jack Leahy, senior metascience adviser at the UK Metascience Unit, warned it should not be seen as a catch-all solution. There is a tendency to be “a little bit evangelical about DPR", Leahy said. "We [the unit] don’t think it is the holy grail. This is just an option—it is a tool in the toolbox that can be used [by] funders.” Leahy said DPR is “certainly not appropriate for every single funding opportunity”, particularly “niche” calls with low application volumes. [Partial randomisation](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-5-ukri-urged-...

Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids By Sophie Hogan Share * [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) * [Share on X](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/&text=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids) * [Share on BlueSky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids%20https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) * [Share on Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/&title=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids&summary=&source=) * [Share via email](mailto:?body=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) *Image source: [ImageryBT](https://stock.adobe.com/uk/images/aerial-view-of-harrogate-convention-centre-and-town-in-north-yorkshire-uk/543688586), via Adobe Stock* **Arma 2026: Stance on generative AI softens, but final funding decisions still taken “by humans”** Research funders in the UK are clarifying their position on generative artificial intelligence to allow its use in the processing of grant applications, members of a group of major funders have said. At the Association for Research Managers and Administrators (Arma) conference on 18 June, representatives from UK Research and Innovation and Wellcome said the Research Funding Policy Group—which includes UKRI and Wellcome alongside six other major funders—will soon update their joint position on the use of generative AI tools in funding applications and assessment. The group released a statement in 2023 which said the use of generative AI “presents potential risks for research”, while individual funders have gone further in their own policies. Wellcome’s policy says “we do not use generative AI tools to assess the quality of grant applications or to aid funding decisions”, while UKRI’s policy says that assessors, including reviewers and panelists, “must not use generative AI tools as part of their assessment activities”. **Shift in stance** But speaking at Arma, Lydia Fulford, senior funding policy manager at UKRI, said the joint statement would be updated such that funders “may use generative AI to process funding applications” but that, “as ever, we will not put a whole application into a publicly available generative AI tool”. Fulford also said the clarified statement will “include that all final funding decisions will be undertaken by humans”. The shift in position by the Research Funding Policy Group—which also includes the Association of Medical Research Charities, the British Heart Foundation, Cancer Research UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Research, the Royal Academy of Engineering and the Royal Society—comes as funders [grapple with skyrocketing volumes](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-4-grant-systems-could-collapse-with-rise-in-ai-driven-demand/) of grant applications. There are suggestions that to tackle such growth in applications, funders could turn to AI, and UKRI has funded a project led by the University of...

LLMs.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # US Government Bans Anthropic Models: National Security or Political Game? ·32·[Technology](/en/technology/) An unexpected turn has occurred in the world of AI: the US government has decided to restrict the use of [Anthropic](/en/tags/anthropic/)'s newest and most powerful models — the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 platforms. While this step was taken from a national security perspective, it is causing significant debate and unexpected consequences in the tech world. This was reported by Techcrunch.com [reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS92aWRlby9pcy10aGUtdXMtZ292ZXJubWVudHMtYW50aHJvcGljLWJhbi1hY2NpZGVudGFsbHktaGVscGluZy10aGUtYnJhbmQv) states. The ban was reportedly triggered by a vulnerability discovered by [Amazon](/en/tags/amazon/) researchers. According to reports, specialists found a way to bypass the guardrails of the Fable 5 model. This could lead to the risk of AI being used for malicious purposes. According to TechCrunch, cybersecurity experts have criticized this decision and issued an open letter. ## Doubts behind the ban and the security issue Anthropic representatives and a group of independent researchers emphasize that these vulnerabilities are not unique to the Fable 5 model. Such "jailbreak" methods currently exist in almost all major language models on the market. Therefore, experts find the government's strict measures specifically against Anthropic products suspicious. Political analysts view this situation as the latest manifestation of the complex relationship between Anthropic and the current Trump administration. TechCrunch journalists discussed on the Equity podcast that political pressure, rather than just security, may be behind this ban. ## Unexpected publicity and IPO prospects Interestingly, this ban could bring unexpected benefits to the Anthropic brand. Typically, technologies deemed "dangerous" and banned at the government level spark a specific interest among investors and users. This is being perceived as an indirect signal that the model is indeed extremely powerful and capable. As Anthropic is currently preparing for its initial public offering (IPO) process, this hype could positively impact the value of the company's shares. For developers and large corporations, the Anthropic platform remains one of the most attractive directions. This situation is also significant for Uzbek specialists and AI enthusiasts. Such global restrictions define how AI technologies will be regulated in the future. The banning of Anthropic models could open new opportunities for competitors like OpenAI and Google, or conversely, start an era of strict censorship for the entire industry. LLMs.titleZh: AI Data Center Boom Stirs Bitcoin Mining Debate - CoinMarketCap
LLMs.content: AI Data Center Boom Stirs Bitcoin Mining Debate Table of Contents Bitcoin News [CMC Crypto News](/academy/categories/cmc-crypto-news) # AI Data Center Boom Stirs Bitcoin Mining Debate [By Decentralized Dog](/academy/author/decentralized-dog) 2m 3mo ago Ran Neuner argued on Sunday that AI has become Bitcoin mining's primary competitor because both industries draw from the same electricity supply #### Table of Contents * [Bitcoin News](#bitcoin-news) ## Bitcoin News A growing number of Bitcoin miners are redirecting resources toward artificial intelligence infrastructure, prompting a debate over what the shift means for network security and Bitcoin's long-term position as a store of value. Crypto trader Ran Neuner argued on Sunday that AI has become Bitcoin mining's primary competitor because both industries draw from the same electricity supply. He said Bitcoin mining generates between $57 and $129 in revenue per megawatt, while AI data centers produce $200 to $500 per megawatt from the same power, making the financial case for switching straightforward. Several major Bitcoin [mining](https://coinmarketcap.com/academy/glossary/mining) companies have already moved in that direction. Core Scientific secured up to $1 billion in credit for AI hosting, MARA Holdings filed with the SEC signaling plans to sell some of its Bitcoin as part of an AI pivot, and Hut 8 signed a $7 billion AI infrastructure agreement with Google in December. Cipher Mining cut its hash rate to focus on AI compute, and Bitmain co-founder Jihan Wu has exited mining entirely in favor of AI. Neuner said Bitcoin's hash rate has fallen 14.5% since its October peak, leaving fewer miners securing the network and raising the theoretical risk of a 51% attack. He argued the situation differs from past bear market drawdowns because the energy that previously returned to Bitcoin mining is now being absorbed by AI infrastructure instead. Not all analysts share that concern. Bitcoin pioneer and cryptographer Adam Back said the network's difficulty adjustment mechanism handles the scenario as designed. He said that the least efficient miners and those switching to AI would exit, profitability for remaining miners would converge toward AI-equivalent returns, and the network would rebalance. Investor Fred Krueger made a similar point, saying miners simply shut off until the difficulty falls far enough to restore profitability. Bitcoin ESG specialist Daniel Batten pushed back on the framing more directly, arguing that AI expansion is actually dependent on Bitcoin rather than the reverse. He said Bitcoin mining's ability to use stranded energy, act as a flexible load balancer for power grids, and operate with older equipment at lower cost gives it structural advantages that AI data centers do not have. Neuner acknowledged that a sustained Bitcoin price recovery could change the calculus for miners considering a switch. He said the asset has posted five consecutive monthly red candles, a stretch not seen since the 2018 bear market, but noted that March is currently tracking an 8% gain, according to CoinGlass data. BitcoinMining hashrate AI revenue per megawatt currently runs up to eight times higher than Bitcoin mining returns, and until that gap narrows, the competitive pressure on miners to redirect their infrastructure is likely to persist. LLMs.titleZh: Legal Innovators Paris, Crosby, LawVu + - Artificial Lawyer
LLMs.content: [## Artificial Lawyer ### Legal Tech & AI News and Views](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/ "Artificial Lawyer") # Legal Innovators Paris, Crosby, LawVu + [19th June 2026](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/2026/06/) [artificiallawyer](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/author/artificiallawyer/) [Wrap](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/category/wrap/) [0](https://www.artificiallawyer.com/2026/06/19/legal-innovators-paris-crosby-lawvu/#respond) After California, where next? Paris of course, for Legal Innovators Europe, on June 24 and 25 in the City of Light, and also the city of great insights into legal AI. Join us next week for the two-day conference with Day One focused on law firms and Day Two for the inhouse world. And if you are at a law firm or inhouse you can gain a free pass via the Express Registration below. **[Legal Innovators Europe](https://www.legalinnovatorseurope.com/) conference in Paris – June 24 (Law Firm Day) and 25 (Inhouse Day).** ***Express route to*[your ticket here.](https://www.legalinnovatorseurope.com/express-registration)** And now for a change, let’s do the videos, then some news. First, a great interview with **Ryan Daniels**, the co-founder of pioneering NewMod Crosby; and also an AL TV Product Walk Through with **LawVu** and their new Draft contract AI capabilities. And, LawVu Draft: OK, and now some news. The Bar of Ireland has chosen Newcode.ai as its exclusive AI provider for all 2,150 members, ‘selected ahead of every competitor, following a half-year pilot with an extensive evaluation process’, they said. Lightbringer, a Swedish startup, has raised €8.5 million in Series A funding to ‘fuel its expansion into the US and accelerate development of its AI-powered patent platform for growing deep tech companies’. LegalFly has launched ‘Collaborator Access’, which enables business teams to ask questions and conduct reviews within guardrails set out by the legal team. Kalisa, an AI knowledge and experience platform, and NuCAS, have announced a partnership. NuCAS will leverage Kalisa’s platform alongside its broader ecosystem of legal technologies to drive greater automation across its managed legal services. Telon, an ‘AI-native services company’, has launched. It’s the creation of Lewis Bretts and Tom Mellor, both former Syke staffers, and has a team of 15. ‘We configure and activate your legal AI stack from the ground up: building agents, engineering prompts, integrating systems, and writing the playbooks that make the platform do what it promised,’ they said. Opus 2 has launched several AI enhancements for use in litigation hearings and international arbitration hearings. They include: ‘Live transcription – Throughout a hearing, the company’s AI-enabled, real-time transcription offering combines Opus 2’s speech-to-text AI with experienced editors to deliver fast, accurate transcripts to all parties, wherever they’re located. And, Testimony insights – As testimony is being transcribed in real time, Opus 2 AI generates insights, such as inconsistencies, by comparing the live evidence being presented with all the documents in the hearing bundle.’ And, MinterEllison has appointed **Pooyan Asgari** as its Chief AI Officer. In this newly created role, they will lead the firm’s AI strategy and drive ‘the responsible, effective integration of AI across the firm’. Asgari joins MinterEllison from Domain Group, where he served as Chief Data Officer for eight years. – And that’s all for now. Have a great weekend and maybe see some of you in Paris next week! P.S. for those of you sending in news and features, AL will be….you guessed it….in France for most of next week and hence will be fairly unkeyboarded from Tuesday to Friday. And it’s going to be HOT….! Luckily, the Pullman Hotel has air-conditioning. **Richard Tromans, Founder, Artificial Lawyer and Legal Innovators chair.** ### Share this: * [Tweet](https://twitter.com/share) * [Click to email a link to a friend (Opens in new window) Email](mailto:?subject...

LLMs.title: Most marketers claim effectiveness becoming ‘easier to prove’ - Marketing Week
LLMs.titleZh: Most marketers claim effectiveness becoming ‘easier to prove’ - Marketing Week
LLMs.content: # Most marketers claim effectiveness becoming ‘easier to prove’ Over the past five years, marketing effectiveness has become easier to prove due to access to rich data and robust analytics, exclusive Marketing Week research suggests. By [Charlotte Rogers](https://www.marketingweek.com/charlotte-rogers/ "Posts by Charlotte Rogers") * [X](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.marketingweek.com/effectiveness-easier-prove/&text=Most marketers claim effectiveness becoming ‘easier to prove’) * [Facebook](https://facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https://www.marketingweek.com/effectiveness-easier-prove/) * [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.marketingweek.com/effectiveness-easier-prove/) Marketing effectiveness is becoming easier to prove thanks to rich data and enhanced capabilities, according to new Marketing Week research. Over half (56.6%) of the more than 630 brand side marketers responding to the 2026 Language of Effectiveness survey find it easier to quantify marketing effectiveness than five years ago. However, at the same time, 15.2% say effectiveness measurement has become harder. Most (58%) CMOs/marketing directors say marketing effectiveness is easier to prove than it was five years ago, an opinion shared by half (50%) of heads of marketing/senior managers/managers. The free text responses from those who claim proving effectiveness is getting easier point to greater access to quality data and analytics tools. Respondents note the ability to measure the impact of “nearly all” paid and organic marketing. Others are benefitting from their senior team taking more of an interest in measurement. [Tech](https://ca.billboard.com/business/tech) # Drake, The Weeknd, Nemahsis, Tre Mission and More Used in AI-Training Databases: Report **The Atlantic** reported that "four giant datasets of songs” — the largest containing 12 million songs — are being shared in the AI music development community, including data from many Canadian acts, including Luna Li, Lunice, Valley and others. [Heather Taylor-Singh](https://ca.billboard.com/author/heathertaylorsingh1) Jun 19, 2026 Nemahsis Norman Wong A new data leak is showing artists if their music has been used to train AI models. Earlier this week, [**The Atlantic**](https://www.theatlantic.com/technology/2026/06/ai-music-generators-suno-google-udio/687485/) published an [AI model-training database](https://www.theatlantic.com/category/ai-watchdog/?) that compiles "four giant datasets of songs that are being shared within the AI-development community” — the two largest containing 12 million tracks. --- It’s not new information that AI-generated databases have been scraping music to train AI music-generating models without proper licensing, permission or compensation for artists. But now artists can see if their music was used for training. Included in the database are songs from Canadian acts like Backxwash, Tre Mission, Lunice, Luna Li, Leith Ross, Valley and Nemahsis, among others. The publication’s tracker highlights exactly which songs are included in the two biggest databases. advertisement A handful of artists have spoken out after finding their music on the database. Rapper and record producer Tre Mission, who has 20 songs on the database, said: “I’m 100% sure I never consented to this 🤔Anyone who knows me, knows I HATE the use of AI in music, so this is very disappointing.” In response to artist Sophia Hjkleightninezeroone — who kicked off the conversation on X about the publication’s database and has 138 entries — Montreal DJ and record producer Lunice discovered that at least 93 of his own works were included. “That’s wild… Thanks for bringing it up,” he said. Some artists put their discovery quite simply: “I dont like this,” Polaris Prize-winning rapper Backxwash wrote [on X](https://x.com/backxwash/status/2067579347985469700?). Following the virality of her post, Hjkleightninezeroone has requested that anyone with connections to lawyers in the music industry get in touch with her. The database also includes songs by some of the country's biggest artists. Drake has almost 800 entries, while The Weeknd notches 460 and Justin Bieber has 458. [Hall of Famer](https://ca.billboard.com/music/awards/juno-awards-2026-winners) Nelly Furtado has 307, whilst Alanis Morissette, Tate McRae and Sarah McLachlan have 259, 210 and 183 entries each, respectively. advertisement Reportedly, the four dataset...

LLMs.title: New OpenAI Method Forecasts AI Risks Before Deployment - BankInfoSecurity
LLMs.titleZh: New OpenAI Method Forecasts AI Risks Before Deployment - BankInfoSecurity
LLMs.content: **TRENDING:** * [The Cybersecurity Skills Gap and the Role of AI for Defenders](/cybersecurity-skills-gap-role-ai-for-defenders-a-31710?rf=tb)• * [Modernizing at Speed: Scaling AI and Apps Without New Silos](/modernizing-at-speed-scaling-ai-apps-without-new-silos-a-31313?rf=TB)• # New OpenAI Method Forecasts AI Risks Before Deployment New Evaluation Method Predicts Harmful AI Behavior Before Launch [Emilia David](https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/authors/emilia-david-i-8064) • June 19, 2026     * [Credit Eligible](/premium/pricing "As a BankInfoSecurity.com annual member, this content can be used toward your membership credits and transcript tracking.") Test before deployment is the careful watchword for artificial intelligence developers. But there's a flaw: testing often relies on synthetic situations that often do not show how AI models or agents actually behave in real-life situations. **See Also:** [AI Impersonation Is the New Arms Race-Is Your Workforce Ready?](https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/ai-impersonation-new-arms-race-is-your-workforce-ready-a-31117?rf=RAM_SeeAlso) A [recent pre-print paper](https://openai.com/index/deployment-simulation/) from OpenAI claims that roughly 99% of the time, its models knew they were being tested. That leaves open a pressing question, the AI firm noted. "If a model is only well behaved because it thinks it's being tested, how do we build confidence that it'll behave safely once it learns it's been deployed?" The company said it found a way around the problem through a method its researchers dub Deployment Simulation, which corrects for sycophantic behavior in which the model "performs" for the evaluator rather than act as it would for a real user. The concept is simple - researchers essentially convinced the models that they were already in production by feeding them a representative sample of real-life prompts, taken from users who opted to have their data used to train new models. They removed the original response and compared the candidate model's answer to what the deployed model said. "Even small, subtle mismatches between evaluation and production environments can produce evaluation awareness," the researchers wrote. One way models realize they’re being tested is because of how obvious the formats of the conversation fed to them were, from exam-style questions to requests that don’t resemble a real conversation. The model picks up the patterns and changes how it behaves to conform to what it thinks the researcher wants to see. The idea is that Deployment Simulation sidesteps that problem by using real user messages because these don’t feature the same types of patterns. Tricking a model into behaving as if it were already in production works pretty well, researchers found. The paper claims that if OpenAI developers had used Deployment Simulation before releasing GPT-5.1, the firm would have spotted a behavior called calculator hacking, in which models uses a browser tool as a calculator to produce a numeric response, instead of actually searching the web. To evaluate the results, the researchers sent data to a GPT-5-Thinking model acting as a grader. The model read each response and categorized it against 20 common misbehavior categories that fall into two major classes - disallowed-content outputs such as sexual content, or "misaligned actions" such as deceiving the user. Based on the rate at which each misbehavior appeared across the responses, the researchers predicted how often these same behaviors would occur once the model is deployed. OpenAI validated these predictions by running the same grading pipeline after the model launched and comparing results to their forecasts. For the new method to work well, it does need access to real, recent ChatGPT user conversations - a dataset only OpenAI has and defeats any prospect of independent auditability. One workaround could exist through WildChat dataset, a publicly available resource for real ChatGPT conversations that occurred b...

Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids By Sophie Hogan Share * [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) * [Share on X](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/&text=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids) * [Share on BlueSky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids%20https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) * [Share on Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/&title=Major UK research funders green light AI for processing grant bids&summary=&source=) * [Share via email](mailto:?body=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-universities-2026-6-major-uk-research-funders-green-light-ai-for-processing-grant-bids/) *Image source: [ImageryBT](https://stock.adobe.com/uk/images/aerial-view-of-harrogate-convention-centre-and-town-in-north-yorkshire-uk/543688586), via Adobe Stock* **Arma 2026: Stance on generative AI softens, but final funding decisions still taken “by humans”** Research funders in the UK are clarifying their position on generative artificial intelligence to allow its use in the processing of grant applications, members of a group of major funders have said. At the Association for Research Managers and Administrators (Arma) conference on 18 June, representatives from UK Research and Innovation and Wellcome said the Research Funding Policy Group—which includes UKRI and Wellcome alongside six other major funders—will soon update their joint position on the use of generative AI tools in funding applications and assessment. The group released a statement in 2023 which said the use of generative AI “presents potential risks for research”, while individual funders have gone further in their own policies. Wellcome’s policy says “we do not use generative AI tools to assess the quality of grant applications or to aid funding decisions”, while UKRI’s policy says that assessors, including reviewers and panelists, “must not use generative AI tools as part of their assessment activities”. **Shift in stance** But speaking at Arma, Lydia Fulford, senior funding policy manager at UKRI, said the joint statement would be updated such that funders “may use generative AI to process funding applications” but that, “as ever, we will not put a whole application into a publicly available generative AI tool”. Fulford also said the clarified statement will “include that all final funding decisions will be undertaken by humans”. The shift in position by the Research Funding Policy Group—which also includes the Association of Medical Research Charities, the British Heart Foundation, Cancer Research UK, the National Institute for Health and Care Research, the Royal Academy of Engineering and the Royal Society—comes as funders [grapple with skyrocketing volumes](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-4-grant-systems-could-collapse-with-rise-in-ai-driven-demand/) of grant applications. There are suggestions that to tackle such growth in applications, funders could turn to AI, and UKRI has funded a project led by the University of...

Variety quotes Minyoung Kim, Netflix VP of content for APAC (ex-India): "I always consider myself and my team as portfolio managers." Variety reports MD Entertainment founder Manoj Punjabi said global streaming services are important gateways for Indonesian stories. Variety reports Media Partners Asia CEO Vivek Couto framed **AI** as having moved from conference talking point to operational reality; JioHotstar chief architect Vijay Seshadri told Variety the platform deployed a conversational voice discovery feature built with OpenAI, with more than **60%** of users choosing voice over text. Panels at APOS covered GenAI, AI-native filmmaking, AI-powered localization and AI video generation, Variety reports. ### What happened Variety reports that the APOS conference in Bali presented eight themes setting the agenda for Asia's entertainment sector in 2026, with an emphasis on **IP**, sport, vertical content and government incentives. Variety quotes Minyoung Kim, Netflix VP of content for APAC (ex-India): "I always consider myself and my team as portfolio managers," while Variety reports MD Entertainment founder Manoj Punjabi described global streaming services as gateways for Indonesian stories. Variety reports Media Partners Asia CEO Vivek Couto opened the summit by framing **AI** as moved from a talking point to operational reality. Variety reports JioHotstar chief architect Vijay Seshadri said the platform has deployed a conversational voice discovery feature built with OpenAI, and that more than **60%** of users now choose voice over text for discovery. Variety reports panels included discussions on GenAI, AI-native filmmaking, AI-powered localization and AI video generation. ### Editorial analysis - technical context Editorial analysis: The shift described at APOS mirrors a common industry progression where generative AI moves from pilot projects into integrated product features such as conversational search, localization pipelines and assistive production tools. Platforms that integrate voice-first discovery and AI-assisted localization typically face engineering workstreams around latency, model governance and dataset localization, and those are the operational challenges practitioners should expect when similar features scale. ### Context and significance For the Asia-Pacific market, the APOS narrative highlights two concurrent dynamics: rising emphasis on **IP** as exportable content across formats, and the embedding of AI across the content value chain. The reported JioHotstar deployment is a concrete example of a streaming platform applying OpenAI-based components to user-facing discovery, which illustrates broader adoption patterns across content platforms rather than a single-vendor technical breakthrough. ### What to watch For practitioners: Track adoption signals such as user engagement metrics for voice discovery (reported **60%** usage at JioHotstar), production workflows adopting AI-native tools, and the emergence of tooling for AI-powered localization and rights-aware asset management. Observers should also watch how publishers and producers operationalize IP as cross-format franchises alongside AI-enabled production and distribution workflows. ## Scoring Rationale The story documents concrete AI deployment signals in APAC streaming and industry-level shifts toward IP-centric franchises. It is notable for practitioners tracking production and discovery tooling, but it is not a frontier-model or re...

Subscribe today to gain access to every *Research Intelligencer* article we publish as well as the exclusive daily newsletter, full access to *The MediaPost Cases*, first-look research and daily insights from Joe Mandese, Editor in Chief. [Become a subscriber today!](/subscribe/None/) # Become a free MediaPost member now to read this article * Unlimited articles every day * Keep up-to-date with media, marketing and advertising news * Invitations to exclusive industry events and research Subscribe to *MediaDailyNews* # Snap Spins Off Gen-AI Video Team To Form New Firm, Dotmo * by [Colin Kirkland](/publications/author/8358/colin-kirkland/) , 17 minutes ago In a move to diversify its AI business operations, Snap is spinning off its generative AI team into a new, independent entity called Dotmo that will focus on the development of artificial intelligence (AI) models and immersive gaming experiences. According to a report from TechCrunch, Dotmo will operate with a license that allows the company to adapt Snap’s proprietary technology for gaming and interactive entertainment platforms.   The new entity will also consist of Snap’s current generative-AI video team, which has decided to technically leave Snap to work on projects unrelated to the social-media platform.  Due to the rising costs of developing AI models and immersive gaming experiences internally, Snap has decided to connect its generative AI video team to a separate company called Dotmo. advertisement advertisement A representative from Snap told TechCrunch that while the planned digital experiences at Dotmo will exist separately from the social media company’s core business, future partnerships with Dotmo may be considered if the fit seems right. Snap will receive a large equity stake in Dotmo, while Dotmo may eventually seek funding outside of Bobby Murphy, Snap CTO and lead investor in Dotmo. Murphy will possess a “significant stake in the new firm,” the report reads. In addition, the report says, “through a financial backer, Murphy will continue to work for Snap full-time as its CTO and continue to lead its GenAI research and development initiatives.” Despite retaining 950 million daily active users while growing its revenue across all markets in Q1 2026, Snap has been struggling with establishing a clearer path to net-income profitability. The company’s stock price plummeted this week after its new generation of [Specs](https://www.mediapost.com/publications/article/415842/snap-unveils-new-gen-ar-glasses-plans-late-2026-l.html) was unveiled, adding another potential challenge in Snap’s long-term revenue plans.  Snap cited high costs in the development of AI models and immersive gaming experiences as a major reason for the creation of its Dotmo spinoff.  If Dotmo is successful, the entity could help cut Snap’s AI-investment costs, while also expanding its creative portfolio. Figma CEO and cofounder Dylan Field said creative people — like the graphic designers who use his company's tools — should find the AI era "a great time to be creative." AI models, he said, are trained on the "distribution of data" and typically create designs that people recognize as "average." Humans, on the other hand, can make something that hasn't been seen before, Field told The New York Times' "Hard Fork" podcast. "If you're in distribution, and you're not actually pushing the bounds, I think that you're in a worse shape than if you're actually going and exploring the frontier of human knowledge, creativity, and what you can put out in the world," Field said. "And making something that's fundamentally new as an expression of yourself. So I get excited about that." Field made the remarks at a San Francisco event hosted by the podcast last week. A video of the interview was posted online on Friday. Figma has released its own AI "vibe design" tools that allow users to mock up apps and other software. It's faced competition from other tech companies, like Google, [which has Stitch](https://www.businessinsider.com/figma-stock-sinks-google-vibe-design-stitch-ai-tool-2026-3), and Anthropic, which has [Claude Design](https://www.businessinsider.com/claude-design-canva-ai-test-compare-create-presentation-saas-2026-5). "In the world of design, I think that what we're going to see and what we are starting to see is a lot more interactivity, a lot more creativity, people really making software more of a creative medium." The CEO also waved away the notion that AI would create a [job apocalypse](https://www.businessinsider.com/figma-ceo-dylan-field-ai-jobs-hiring-2025-10) for graphic designers. Jobs will become [more generalist](https://www.businessinsider.com/ai-empowering-generalist-behaviour-figma-ceo-tech-design-2025-8) rather than specialized, he said. "A lot of people that are doing other jobs, I think, will start calling themselves 'designers-creatives," Field said. AI Product.titleZh: Indeavor Launches AI Analytics Hub to Turn Frontline Workforce Scheduling and Absence Data Into Real-Time Insights With AI - HRTech Series
AI Product.content: * [Likes](https://www.facebook.com/TecHR-Series-235148877438730refbookmarks) * [Followers](https://twitter.com/HRTechSeries) [TecHR - TecHR Series covers news,views and interviews from the HR technology realm](https://techrseries.com/) # Indeavor Launches AI Analytics Hub to Turn Frontline Workforce Scheduling and Absence Data Into Real-Time Insights With AI [HR](https://techrseries.com/category/hr/)[Artificial Intelligence](https://techrseries.com/category/artificial-intelligence/)[News](https://techrseries.com/category/news/) [By **Business Wire**](https://techrseries.com/author/business-wire/ "Browse Author Articles") [Indeavor](https://www.indeavor.com/), a leader in AI-powered shift scheduling for complex 24/7 operations, announced the launch of AI Analytics Hub, a natural language reporting platform that lets users get instant workforce insights using plain English instead of SQL or spreadsheets. **Catch more HRTech Insights: [HRTech Interview With Hari Kolam, CEO and Co-founder of Findem: Featuring Findem’s GliderAI](https://techrseries.com/interviews/hrtech-interview-with-hari-kolam-ceo-and-co-founder-of-findem-featuring-findems-gliderai/)** In high-stakes industries like manufacturing, food and beverage, energy, and nuclear, operations leaders have long relied on manual exports, pivot tables, and IT support to answer critical workforce questions. The AI Analytics Hub eliminates this friction by connecting directly to an organization’s scheduling and absence data and delivering interactive dashboards, reports, and benchmarks. No coding required. “Site leaders, HR, and enterprise leadership finally have the real-time frontline workforce visibility they’ve been missing,” said Brandon Schwarz, CEO of Indeavor. “The AI Analytics Hub allows anyone in the organization to easily access a range of information, all through simple prompts – such as ‘Compare my absenteeism trends across facilities for the past month, or show me production department overtime for last week’ – all on demand and easily accessible without any internal technical help or knowledge needed. It’s a game changer for providing decision makers real-time, on-the-ground analytics, reports, and data without the burden of a BI implementation.” **Built for Real-World Operational Decisions** The AI Analytics Hub goes beyond generic BI tools. It is purpose-built for Indeavor scheduling data and instantly delivers the metrics leaders actually need in 24/7 regulated environments. Key Capabilities Include: * Instant Answers in Plain English: Turn complex workforce questions into interactive reports and charts in seconds without SQL, spreadsheets, or IT support. * Built for Indeavor Data: Connects directly to the organization’s scheduling and absence data with no custom integrations required. * Smart Insights: Use AI to analyze your data and surface trends, risks, and recommendations (e.g., overtime spikes or staffing gaps). * Automated Delivery: Schedule dashboards and reports to be delivered via email on a defined cadence. * Benchmarking and Standardization: Compare performance across sites and domains using consistent data definitions and industry benchmarks. * Self-Service Access for Leaders: Enable site managers, HR, and enterprise leadership to access insights directly without relying on analysts. **From Manual Reporting to Real-Time Intelligence** With AI Analytics Hub, site leaders gain immediate cross-site visibility into overtime, absenteeism, and staffing trends. HR and operations leaders can build custom reports and receive automated updates without IT dependency. Executives get standardized, formatted dashboards delivered automatically – enabling faster, data-driven decisions across the enterprise. **Read More on Hrtech : [Why SWIFT is Too Slow for Your Global Workforce?](https://techrseries.com/featured/why-swift-is-too-slow-for-your-global-workforce/)** **[To share your insights with us, please write to**[**psen@itechseries.com**](mailto:psen@itechseries...
