AI Product.title: Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation - Telecoms
AI Product.titleZh: Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation - Telecoms
AI Product.content: * [AI](/digital-ecosystem/ai) * [Digital Ecosystem](/digital-ecosystem) * [News](/latest-news) # Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation Mavenir and Red Hat have unveiled a new platform that essentially enables direct carrier billing for AI services. [Nick Wood,](/author/nick-wood) Freelance writer June 19, 2026 3 Min Read They call it an Integrated AI Platform, and it brings together network software maker Mavenir's AI software platform and open source specialist Red Hat's enterprise Kubernetes and AI capabilities to provide a carrier-grade, on-premises, cloud native AI infrastructure platform. Once up and running, Mavenir says operators can monetise AI similarly to how they monetise data – by creating token-based consumption plans with full control over pricing and service level agreements (SLAs), with the fees added as an item on the customer's phone bill. Mavenir and Red Hat say their platform supports three operating models. It can be used as an enterprise AI platform where the operator offers metered access to AI models, compute resources and AI tooling to customers as a value-added service alongside core connectivity services. It can also function as AI grid infrastructure, where operators use the platform to charge customers for hosting AI applications and third-party workloads. Related: [Nokia plots 10-fold capacity increase at US chip facility](/ai/nokia-plots-10-fold-capacity-increase-at-us-chip-facility) Finally, operators can use the platform to offer their own AI products and services to their subscribers, including AI assistant services, billed on a token-consumption basis. The platform has been designed with on-premises AI models and small language models in mind; however, it also comes with the option of facilitating policy-governed access to external frontier models when enterprises are in need of a bit more oomph. "Operators are watching AI revenues flow to hyperscalers and third-party platforms while they provide the connectivity that makes it all possible. This changes today. The Integrated AI Platform developed in collaboration between Mavenir and Red Hat gives operators the infrastructure to become AI service providers in their own right," said Mavenir's chief technology and strategy officer, Bejoy Pankajakshan. "Operators gain sovereign control over models and data, token-accurate monetisation that integrates with their existing BSS, and the service assurance to back their own managed AI services with contractual SLAs. Whether operators are launching subscriber AI services, building out their AI grid infrastructure, or offering AI capabilities to enterprise customers on a consumption basis, the platform provides the commercial and operational controls to do it at carrier scale." Chris Wright, CTO and SVP of global engineering at Red Hat, chimed in with: "Working with Mavenir, we're delivering an integrated solution supported on the Kubernetes-native foundation powered by Red Hat AI, which brings MLOps, vLLM inference and AgentOps capabilities. Related: [AI app disruption is on the up](/ai/ai-app-disruption-is-on-the-up) "This hybrid architecture allows operators to run sovereign on-premises models for the bulk of their traffic and selectively connect to frontier models for tasks that require it, all through a single policy and billing framework." Keeping close tabs on access to external frontier models is likely to strike a chord with potential customers. The recent shift by the AI industry from flat-rate to consumption-based pricing models has echoes of the bill shocks experienced by end users in the early, heady days of metered mobile data usage. High-profile examples are in ample supply. The most oft-referenced one is Uber CTO Neppalli Naga telling  [The Information](https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/2ahACXD7MEUG9w4rkI6fncW0o6U?domain=theinformation.com) [(paywall)](https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/2ahACXD7MEUG9w4rkI6fncW0o6U?domain=theinformation.com)  that his company had...

Open Source AI.titleZh: New OpenAI Method Forecasts AI Risks Before Deployment - BankInfoSecurity
Open Source AI.content: **TRENDING:** * [The Cybersecurity Skills Gap and the Role of AI for Defenders](/cybersecurity-skills-gap-role-ai-for-defenders-a-31710?rf=tb)• * [Modernizing at Speed: Scaling AI and Apps Without New Silos](/modernizing-at-speed-scaling-ai-apps-without-new-silos-a-31313?rf=TB)• # New OpenAI Method Forecasts AI Risks Before Deployment New Evaluation Method Predicts Harmful AI Behavior Before Launch [Emilia David](https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/authors/emilia-david-i-8064) • June 19, 2026     * [Credit Eligible](/premium/pricing "As a BankInfoSecurity.com annual member, this content can be used toward your membership credits and transcript tracking.") Test before deployment is the careful watchword for artificial intelligence developers. But there's a flaw: testing often relies on synthetic situations that often do not show how AI models or agents actually behave in real-life situations. **See Also:** [AI Impersonation Is the New Arms Race-Is Your Workforce Ready?](https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/ai-impersonation-new-arms-race-is-your-workforce-ready-a-31117?rf=RAM_SeeAlso) A [recent pre-print paper](https://openai.com/index/deployment-simulation/) from OpenAI claims that roughly 99% of the time, its models knew they were being tested. That leaves open a pressing question, the AI firm noted. "If a model is only well behaved because it thinks it's being tested, how do we build confidence that it'll behave safely once it learns it's been deployed?" The company said it found a way around the problem through a method its researchers dub Deployment Simulation, which corrects for sycophantic behavior in which the model "performs" for the evaluator rather than act as it would for a real user. The concept is simple - researchers essentially convinced the models that they were already in production by feeding them a representative sample of real-life prompts, taken from users who opted to have their data used to train new models. They removed the original response and compared the candidate model's answer to what the deployed model said. "Even small, subtle mismatches between evaluation and production environments can produce evaluation awareness," the researchers wrote. One way models realize they’re being tested is because of how obvious the formats of the conversation fed to them were, from exam-style questions to requests that don’t resemble a real conversation. The model picks up the patterns and changes how it behaves to conform to what it thinks the researcher wants to see. The idea is that Deployment Simulation sidesteps that problem by using real user messages because these don’t feature the same types of patterns. Tricking a model into behaving as if it were already in production works pretty well, researchers found. The paper claims that if OpenAI developers had used Deployment Simulation before releasing GPT-5.1, the firm would have spotted a behavior called calculator hacking, in which models uses a browser tool as a calculator to produce a numeric response, instead of actually searching the web. To evaluate the results, the researchers sent data to a GPT-5-Thinking model acting as a grader. The model read each response and categorized it against 20 common misbehavior categories that fall into two major classes - disallowed-content outputs such as sexual content, or "misaligned actions" such as deceiving the user. Based on the rate at which each misbehavior appeared across the responses, the researchers predicted how often these same behaviors would occur once the model is deployed. OpenAI validated these predictions by running the same grading pipeline after the model launched and comparing results to their forecasts. For the new method to work well, it does need access to real, recent ChatGPT user conversations - a dataset only OpenAI has and defeats any prospect of independent auditability. One workaround could exist through WildChat dataset, a publicly available resource for real ChatGPT conversations that occurred b...

Open Source AI.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # US Government Bans Anthropic Models: National Security or Political Game? ·32·[Technology](/en/technology/) An unexpected turn has occurred in the world of AI: the US government has decided to restrict the use of [Anthropic](/en/tags/anthropic/)'s newest and most powerful models — the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 platforms. While this step was taken from a national security perspective, it is causing significant debate and unexpected consequences in the tech world. This was reported by Techcrunch.com [reports](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS92aWRlby9pcy10aGUtdXMtZ292ZXJubWVudHMtYW50aHJvcGljLWJhbi1hY2NpZGVudGFsbHktaGVscGluZy10aGUtYnJhbmQv) states. The ban was reportedly triggered by a vulnerability discovered by [Amazon](/en/tags/amazon/) researchers. According to reports, specialists found a way to bypass the guardrails of the Fable 5 model. This could lead to the risk of AI being used for malicious purposes. According to TechCrunch, cybersecurity experts have criticized this decision and issued an open letter. ## Doubts behind the ban and the security issue Anthropic representatives and a group of independent researchers emphasize that these vulnerabilities are not unique to the Fable 5 model. Such "jailbreak" methods currently exist in almost all major language models on the market. Therefore, experts find the government's strict measures specifically against Anthropic products suspicious. Political analysts view this situation as the latest manifestation of the complex relationship between Anthropic and the current Trump administration. TechCrunch journalists discussed on the Equity podcast that political pressure, rather than just security, may be behind this ban. ## Unexpected publicity and IPO prospects Interestingly, this ban could bring unexpected benefits to the Anthropic brand. Typically, technologies deemed "dangerous" and banned at the government level spark a specific interest among investors and users. This is being perceived as an indirect signal that the model is indeed extremely powerful and capable. As Anthropic is currently preparing for its initial public offering (IPO) process, this hype could positively impact the value of the company's shares. For developers and large corporations, the Anthropic platform remains one of the most attractive directions. This situation is also significant for Uzbek specialists and AI enthusiasts. Such global restrictions define how AI technologies will be regulated in the future. The banning of Anthropic models could open new opportunities for competitors like OpenAI and Google, or conversely, start an era of strict censorship for the entire industry. Open Source AI.content: [Asosiy kontentga oʻtish](#main) * [Uzbekistan](/en/uzbekistan/) * [World](/en/world/) * [Society](/en/society/) * [Tech](/en/technology/) * [Sport](/en/sport/) * [Auto](/en/auto/) * [Culture](/en/culture/) * [Economy](/en/economy/) * [Ўзбекча](#) * [O'zbek](#) * [Русский](#) * [Türkçe](#) * [Қазақша](#) * [Français](#) * [Español](#) * [Deutsch](#) # AI and National Security: US Bans Export of Anthropic Models ·2·[Technology](/en/technology/) The US government has ordered [Anthropic](/en/tags/anthropic/) to restrict the export of its most powerful AI models — Fable and Mythos — due to national security concerns. This ban applies not only to foreign states but also to foreign citizens within the US. Following this unexpected decision, the AI giant completely removed both models from use, and they have been unavailable to anyone for a week. This is reported by Techcrunch.com [report](https://zamin.uz/index.php?do=go&url=aHR0cHM6Ly90ZWNoY3J1bmNoLmNvbS8yMDI2LzA2LzE5L2VuY3J5cHRpb24tc3B5d2FyZS1hbmQtbm93LW15dGhvcy1oaXN0b3J5LXNob3dzLXdoeS1jeWJlci1leHBvcnQtY29udHJvbC1kb2VzbnQtd29yay8%3D) says. This event is a unique test for the US government. Whether the Washington administration can curb advanced AI technologies through export control mechanisms remains to be seen. Previous similar attempts related to encryption technologies and spyware did not yield the expected results. According to ixbt.com, this conflict could set new rules that not only Anthropic but all other AI laboratories must follow. ## Why were Mythos and Fable deemed dangerous? When Anthropic introduced the Mythos model in April, it described it as a powerful tool that could cause significant devastation in cyberspace. Therefore, even before the ban, access to this model was granted only to 150 vetted companies and government organizations. The goal was to help defenders strengthen their software before malicious actors could gain such capabilities. It is said that two main events triggered the activation of the ban. First, Anthropic allowed South Korea's SK Telecom operator to use the Mythos model. US officials suspect the company's ties to China, although SK Telecom has denied these claims. Second, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy warned the government that a way to bypass the protection systems in the Fable 5 model had been found. While Anthropic called this error minor, the US Department of Commerce immediately issued an export control directive. ## Historical Experience: Does Export Control Work? Governments' attempts to limit the spread of cyber technologies are not new, but history shows few successes in this regard. In the 1990s, the US government viewed the PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) encryption software as a weapon and opened a criminal case against its creator, Phil Zimmermann. However, Zimmermann published the program code in book form, placing it under freedom of speech protection, which paved the way for the encryption technologies used today in messengers like Signal and WhatsApp. Additionally, in the early 2010s, after it became known that Western-made spyware was used against dissidents in the Middle East, the Wassenaar Arrangement was expanded internationally. This agreement aims to restrict the export of technologies that can be used for both civilian and military purposes. The situation with Anthropic is the next important step in showing how effective these methods are in the age of AI. Open Source AI.content: #### While other MediaPost newsletters and articles remain free to all ... our new Research Intelligencer service is reserved for paid subscribers ... Subscribe today to gain access to every *Research Intelligencer* article we publish as well as the exclusive daily newsletter, full access to *The MediaPost Cases*, first-look research and daily insights from Joe Mandese, Editor in Chief. [Become a subscriber today!](/subscribe/None/) # Become a free MediaPost member now to read this article * Unlimited articles every day * Keep up-to-date with media, marketing and advertising news * Invitations to exclusive industry events and research Subscribe to *MediaDailyNews* # Snap Spins Off Gen-AI Video Team To Form New Firm, Dotmo * by [Colin Kirkland](/publications/author/8358/colin-kirkland/) , 17 minutes ago In a move to diversify its AI business operations, Snap is spinning off its generative AI team into a new, independent entity called Dotmo that will focus on the development of artificial intelligence (AI) models and immersive gaming experiences. According to a report from TechCrunch, Dotmo will operate with a license that allows the company to adapt Snap’s proprietary technology for gaming and interactive entertainment platforms.   The new entity will also consist of Snap’s current generative-AI video team, which has decided to technically leave Snap to work on projects unrelated to the social-media platform.  Due to the rising costs of developing AI models and immersive gaming experiences internally, Snap has decided to connect its generative AI video team to a separate company called Dotmo. advertisement advertisement A representative from Snap told TechCrunch that while the planned digital experiences at Dotmo will exist separately from the social media company’s core business, future partnerships with Dotmo may be considered if the fit seems right. Snap will receive a large equity stake in Dotmo, while Dotmo may eventually seek funding outside of Bobby Murphy, Snap CTO and lead investor in Dotmo. Murphy will possess a “significant stake in the new firm,” the report reads. In addition, the report says, “through a financial backer, Murphy will continue to work for Snap full-time as its CTO and continue to lead its GenAI research and development initiatives.” Despite retaining 950 million daily active users while growing its revenue across all markets in Q1 2026, Snap has been struggling with establishing a clearer path to net-income profitability. The company’s stock price plummeted this week after its new generation of [Specs](https://www.mediapost.com/publications/article/415842/snap-unveils-new-gen-ar-glasses-plans-late-2026-l.html) was unveiled, adding another potential challenge in Snap’s long-term revenue plans.  Snap cited high costs in the development of AI models and immersive gaming experiences as a major reason for the creation of its Dotmo spinoff.  If Dotmo is successful, the entity could help cut Snap’s AI-investment costs, while also expanding its creative portfolio. Murphy will possess a “significant stake in the new firm,” the report reads. In addition, the report says, “through a financial backer, Murphy will continue to work for Snap full-time as its CTO and continue to lead its GenAI research and development initiatives.” Despite retaining 950 million daily active users while growing its revenue across all markets in Q1 2026, Snap has been struggling with establishing a clearer path to net-income profitability. The company’s stock price plummeted this week after its new generation of [Specs](https://www.mediapost.com/publications/article/415842/snap-unveils-new-gen-ar-glasses-plans-late-2026-l.html) was unveiled, adding another potential challenge in Snap’s long-term revenue plans.  Snap cited high costs in the development of AI models and immersive gaming experiences as a major reason for the creation of its Dotmo spinoff.  If Dotmo is successful, the entity could help cut Snap’s AI-investment costs, while also expanding its creative portfolio. Open Source AI.content: [Skip to content](#main-content) [LDS Let's Data ScienceLEARN • BUILD • STAY AHEAD](/) [Sign in](/login?redirect=%2Fnews%2Frunway-showcases-ai-generated-shorts-to-packed-audience-3825c8ad) Industry Applicationsrunwayai videogenerative mediaentertainment # Runway Showcases AI-Generated Shorts to Packed Audience | 5.8 Relevance Score Runway's annual AI Festival filled Alice Tully Hall at New York's Lincoln Center on June 11, screening 10 short films chosen from thousands of entrants. The Grand Prix went to Robert Gaudette, a 54-year-old Toronto nonprofit worker with no formal film training, for 'A Face Only a Mother Could Love' -- an eight-minute Paris-set drama that earned a $50,000 prize and drew strong audience reaction. Oscar-winning director Ron Howard attended and gave a fireside chat with co-CEO Cristobal Valenzuela, saying the technology is 'democratizing' filmmaking but that audiences will ultimately determine what succeeds: 'What's worth our time? What are we invested in?' Business Insider's reporter described many films as visually near-human but called several 'boring,' while the hall drew an enthusiastic crowd. An LA installment at The Broad Stage Theater follows on June 18. ### Grand Prix and Event Overview Runway's fourth annual AI Festival -- rebranded from the AI Film Festival to reflect expanded categories including Design, Fashion, Advertising, Gaming, and New Media -- screened 10 short films at Alice Tully Hall in Lincoln Center on June 11, 2026, chosen from thousands of entrants. The $50,000 Grand Prix went to Robert Gaudette, a 54-year-old nonprofit worker from Toronto with no formal film training, for 'A Face Only a Mother Could Love,' an eight-minute AI-generated short about a Parisian man with a facial disfigurement who dances nightly awaiting a companion that never arrives. The Hollywood Reporter described the crowd as 'practically erupting' when Gaudette's win was announced. Gaudette told THR he worked alone with no actors or crew over two weeks of 17-hour days. An LA screening follows at The Broad Stage Theater on June 18. ### Ron Howard Fireside Chat Oscar-winning director Ron Howard attended and gave a fireside chat with Runway co-CEO Cristobal Valenzuela. Howard said the technology has been 'democratizing' filmmaking but said audience appetite will determine commercial outcomes: 'It's going to be, again, up to the audiences to determine what appeals, what resonates,' Howard said, per Variety. 'It's going to ultimately be determined by us. What's worth our time? What are we invested in?' Howard also said he has not yet seen the efficiencies AI is said to deliver in his own production work: 'It seemed it's going to create a lot of efficiencies, but so far I can't say that I've seen it yet in my world.' Valenzuela, at a press conference before the Howard chat, pushed back on zero-sum framing: 'This is not a zero-sum game. The human is the one generating the content in the first place,' per Variety. ### What the Films Revealed Business Insider's reporter described many films as visually indistinguishable from human-made media but called several 'boring,' a pattern consistent with generative video's recurring tension between technical fidelity and narrative craft. The Grand Prix winner stands as a counterexample -- Gaudette constructed scenes lasting 30 seconds or longer by running large numbers of model generations until shots matched, a deliberate and labor-intensive approach THR credited for the film's emotional coherence. A French summer-childhood short, 'Costa Verde,' was also noted by THR for leaning into AI imagery's visual qualities to distinctive stylistic effect. ### Company Context Runway has raised more than $800 million in total funding, including a $308 million Series D in spring 2025 that valued the company at $3 billion, per Deadline. The 2025 festival resulted in an IMAX deal that brought festival shorts to commercial screens in 10 U.S. cities. The 2026 edition expanded to include Desig...

Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says By Isaac Barbosa Share * [Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/) * [Share on X](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/&text=Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says) * [Share on BlueSky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says%20https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/) * [Share on Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/&title=Distributed peer review ‘good but no holy grail’, Dsit official says&summary=&source=) * [Share via email](mailto:?body=https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-6-distributed-peer-review-good-but-no-holy-grail-dsit-official-says/) *Image source: [Paper Trident](https://www.gettyimages.co.uk/detail/illustration/people-sitting-around-round-table-top-view-royalty-free-illustration/1944763144), via Getty Images* **Arma 2026: Dsit-UKRI metascience unit also plans survey of how and why researchers use AI** A radical peer review programme that uses grant applicants as reviewers is promising but not “the holy grail” for the research sector, a metascience adviser at the Department for Science, Information and Technology has said. He also revealed that a survey of artificial intelligence use across the research system is in the works. Unlike traditional peer review methods, which rely on external volunteers, distributed peer review (DPR) requires that grant applicants review others on the same funding call. Scores are designed to have no bearing on the ranking of the reviewer’s own application. The method has been examined by the UK Metascience Unit—a joint venture between Dsit and UK Research and Innovation—and widely praised. A UKRI trial last year found that DPR shortened the assessment time of applications by [up to 65 per cent](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2025-7-ukri-to-scale-up-radical-change-to-peer-review/) compared with the funder’s typical calls, leading to it being rolled out across some major funding calls across research councils. **Misplaced evangelism?** At a session of the Association of Research Managers and Administrators conference on 18 June, DPR was presented as a potential response to pressures facing the research sector, including [soaring grant application volumes](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-5-funders-scramble-to-limit-demand-as-research-grant-applications-rocket/). However, Jack Leahy, senior metascience adviser at the UK Metascience Unit, warned it should not be seen as a catch-all solution. There is a tendency to be “a little bit evangelical about DPR", Leahy said. "We [the unit] don’t think it is the holy grail. This is just an option—it is a tool in the toolbox that can be used [by] funders.” Leahy said DPR is “certainly not appropriate for every single funding opportunity”, particularly “niche” calls with low application volumes. [Partial randomisation](https://www.researchprofessionalnews.com/rr-news-uk-research-councils-2026-5-ukri-urged-...

Infra & Chips.content: [Skip to content](#main-content) [LDS Let's Data ScienceLEARN • BUILD • STAY AHEAD](/) [Sign in](/login?redirect=%2Fnews%2Famazon-explores-selling-trainium-chips-to-data-centres-b86b4fe9) Infrastructureamazontrainiumai chipsnvidia competition # Amazon explores selling Trainium chips to data centres | 7.0 Relevance Score Amazon is exploring selling its custom **Trainium** AI chips to third-party data centres, according to reporting by Bloomberg and TechCrunch. AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg that AWS is in talks to allow other organisations to use Trainium outside of AWS. The conversations are described in coverage as early-stage, per TechCrunch. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy wrote in his April shareholder letter that, if the chip business operated standalone and sold externally, it could reach an annual run rate of around **$50 billion**, a figure cited by TechCrunch and India Today. Reporting frames the move as a more direct challenge to **Nvidia** for AI infrastructure capacity, while sources note existing production and allocation constraints for Trainium capacity. ### What happened Amazon is exploring external sales of its custom AI chip family, **Trainium**, for use in other companies' data centres, according to reporting by Bloomberg and TechCrunch. AWS AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg that AWS is in talks to allow third parties to use Trainium in their own data centres, per TechCrunch and Bloomberg. TechCrunch and India Today report the discussions are at an early stage. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy wrote in his April shareholder letter that a standalone chips business selling to AWS and external customers could reach an annual run rate of around **$50 billion**, a figure cited in public coverage by TechCrunch and India Today. Public reporting also frames the proposal as a potential competitive move against **Nvidia**, which currently dominates AI accelerator sales, per TechCrunch and Bloomberg. ### Editorial analysis - technical context Companies selling custom AI accelerators outside their own clouds introduces both supply-chain and integration challenges. Industry-pattern observations: vendors that supply chips to third-party data centres typically need to secure high-volume manufacturing capacity from foundry partners such as **TSMC**, handle firmware/driver support, and provide system-level integration guidance for rack-level deployments. Industry-pattern observations: cloud providers that internalise accelerators and then externalise sales must balance allocation between retail cloud customers and external purchasers, which can create short-term capacity constraints and lead times for compute-hungry customers. ### Context and significance If realised at scale, third-party sales of cloud-designed accelerators reshape where hyperscalers exert influence over the server and rack market and increase vendor choices for organisations building localized AI infrastructure. Reporting frames Amazon's discussions as a direct competitive pressure on **Nvidia**, but sources indicate the effort is nascent and constrained by current production and allocation dynamics. ### Industry context Andy Jassy's **$50 billion** run-rate figure, reported in his shareholder letter and cited by multiple outlets, signals the revenue potential Amazon attributes to hardware sales if the business model changes; that figure is a public claim in Amazon's own shareholder communication, not an independent forecast. ### What to watch * •Production and supply: observers should monitor announcements or filings that name foundry partners or capacity expansion deals, which would indicate the feasibility of large-scale external sales. * •Sales model and packaging: whether Amazon offers standalone chips, full servers, or rack systems will affect total cost of ownership for buyers and competitive positioning versus vendors that sell both accelerators and integrated systems. * •Third-party uptake and contracts: named customer deals or pilot deployments repor...

Infra & Chips.titleZh: Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation - Telecoms
Infra & Chips.content: * [AI](/digital-ecosystem/ai) * [Digital Ecosystem](/digital-ecosystem) * [News](/latest-news) # Mavenir and Red Hat set their sights on AI monetisation Mavenir and Red Hat have unveiled a new platform that essentially enables direct carrier billing for AI services. [Nick Wood,](/author/nick-wood) Freelance writer June 19, 2026 3 Min Read They call it an Integrated AI Platform, and it brings together network software maker Mavenir's AI software platform and open source specialist Red Hat's enterprise Kubernetes and AI capabilities to provide a carrier-grade, on-premises, cloud native AI infrastructure platform. Once up and running, Mavenir says operators can monetise AI similarly to how they monetise data – by creating token-based consumption plans with full control over pricing and service level agreements (SLAs), with the fees added as an item on the customer's phone bill. Mavenir and Red Hat say their platform supports three operating models. It can be used as an enterprise AI platform where the operator offers metered access to AI models, compute resources and AI tooling to customers as a value-added service alongside core connectivity services. It can also function as AI grid infrastructure, where operators use the platform to charge customers for hosting AI applications and third-party workloads. Related: [Nokia plots 10-fold capacity increase at US chip facility](/ai/nokia-plots-10-fold-capacity-increase-at-us-chip-facility) Finally, operators can use the platform to offer their own AI products and services to their subscribers, including AI assistant services, billed on a token-consumption basis. The platform has been designed with on-premises AI models and small language models in mind; however, it also comes with the option of facilitating policy-governed access to external frontier models when enterprises are in need of a bit more oomph. "Operators are watching AI revenues flow to hyperscalers and third-party platforms while they provide the connectivity that makes it all possible. This changes today. The Integrated AI Platform developed in collaboration between Mavenir and Red Hat gives operators the infrastructure to become AI service providers in their own right," said Mavenir's chief technology and strategy officer, Bejoy Pankajakshan. "Operators gain sovereign control over models and data, token-accurate monetisation that integrates with their existing BSS, and the service assurance to back their own managed AI services with contractual SLAs. Whether operators are launching subscriber AI services, building out their AI grid infrastructure, or offering AI capabilities to enterprise customers on a consumption basis, the platform provides the commercial and operational controls to do it at carrier scale." Chris Wright, CTO and SVP of global engineering at Red Hat, chimed in with: "Working with Mavenir, we're delivering an integrated solution supported on the Kubernetes-native foundation powered by Red Hat AI, which brings MLOps, vLLM inference and AgentOps capabilities. Related: [AI app disruption is on the up](/ai/ai-app-disruption-is-on-the-up) "This hybrid architecture allows operators to run sovereign on-premises models for the bulk of their traffic and selectively connect to frontier models for tasks that require it, all through a single policy and billing framework." Keeping close tabs on access to external frontier models is likely to strike a chord with potential customers. The recent shift by the AI industry from flat-rate to consumption-based pricing models has echoes of the bill shocks experienced by end users in the early, heady days of metered mobile data usage. High-profile examples are in ample supply. The most oft-referenced one is Uber CTO Neppalli Naga telling  [The Information](https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/2ahACXD7MEUG9w4rkI6fncW0o6U?domain=theinformation.com) [(paywall)](https://url.us.m.mimecastprotect.com/s/2ahACXD7MEUG9w4rkI6fncW0o6U?domain=theinformation.com)  that his company had...

Infra & Chips.title: Micron Earnings Seen as Key Test for AI-Driven Stock Market Rally - Global Banking & Finance Review
Infra & Chips.titleZh: Micron Earnings Seen as Key Test for AI-Driven Stock Market Rally - Global Banking & Finance Review
Infra & Chips.content: [Finance](/category/finance/) # Investors see Micron earnings as pulse check of AI rally momentum Published by [Global Banking & Finance Review](/author/global-banking-and-finance-review/) Posted on June 19, 2026 4 min read · Last updated: June 19, 2026 [Add as preferred source on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=globalbankingandfinance.com) [Finance](/tag/finance/) [Banking](/tag/banking/) [Markets](/tag/markets/) [technology](/tag/technology/) [Investing](/tag/investing/) ## Micron Earnings Under Spotlight as Investors Gauge AI Rally Momentum By Laura Matthews ## Micron Earnings and the AI-Fueled Stock Market Rally NEW YORK, June 19 (Reuters) - Investors are seeking signs that the U.S. stock market rally fueled by artificial intelligence has more life left in it, and the upcoming Micron Technology earnings will check the pulse of chip demand to see if it is still accelerating. Despite a sharp mid-week selloff, major U.S. stock indexes are hovering near all-time highs, supported by robust corporate earnings driven by an AI investment boom and relief from the Iran war. ### Micron’s Performance and Investor Expectations Micron's shares are up 298% this year, and the memory chip maker's quarterly report on Wednesday, June 24, will help investors gauge whether the surge in spending on data centers and the resulting profits generated across the semiconductor sector can continue to surprise to the upside. #### Market Momentum and Analyst Insights "There’s been a lot of momentum here recently," said Andy Pratt, director of investment strategy at Burney Company. "This AI trend is something that’s continued, and honestly, what we see with this revenue surprise signal that we monitor is there's still a lot of juice." ##### Industry Partnerships and Broader Market Impact Apple has agreed to partner with Intel to design and manufacture chips in the U.S., which could significantly boost the chipmaker's turnaround efforts. That helped to lift the S&P 500 nearly 1% so far this week, on pace for a second weekly gain. Meanwhile, the Philadelphia SE Semiconductor index hit a record high and was last up 7% for the week. ### Looking for Reinforcements The stakes are high. Micron's earnings come at a time when valuations are elevated and investors are questioning whether the rally is overextended. Any indication of underlying demand and continued AI-related spending strength could give investors confidence to keep stoking the rally. Micron's earnings are "setting up as a classic positive feedback loop," said Steve Kolano, chief investment officer at Integrated Partners. "That really seems to be kind of the only game in town. ... If you look at the book to bill of semiconductor companies right now and the backlog, the demand is just through the roof in relation to chip capacity." Big Tech has signaled that AI spending is not slowing, set to rise past $700 billion this year from $400 billion in 2025. ### Macro Backdrop Still Looms Although the AI narrative has dominated markets, underlying macroeconomic concerns remain. The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation measure is due next week. So, too, is a final reading on first-quarter GDP. Both reports will provide checks on the health of the U.S. consumer and economic growth. #### Earnings Growth and Market Strategy Second-quarter earnings growth for the S&P 500 is estimated at 22.9%, down from 29.3% in the first quarter, according to data provided by Tajinder Dhillon, head of earnings research at LSEG. Drew Matus, chief market strategist at MetLife Investment Management, said strong equity markets have been one of the main supports for consumers, and anything that challenges the AI trade or the continued rise in stocks is being closely watched. ##### Wealth Effect and Future Outlook "It has not just been market effects but macroeconomic effects at this point," he said. "We’re definitely worried about the wealth effect going away and what that might mean." #### AI Trade Momentum and...

Infra & Chips.title: UW to launch new AI minor as universities scramble to meet workforce demand - KIRO 7 News Seattle
Infra & Chips.titleZh: UW to launch new AI minor as universities scramble to meet workforce demand - KIRO 7 News Seattle
Infra & Chips.content: * [News](/news) * [PinPoint Weather](/weather) * [KIRO 7 Investigates](/news/investigates) * [Sports](/sports) * [Contact Us](/station/contact-us) # UW to launch new AI minor as universities scramble to meet workforce demand By [Julia Dallas](/author/julia-dallas/) The University of Washington (UW) is looking to keep students on the cutting edge of technology as artificial intelligence continues to reshape industries across the board. UW is set to launch a new interdisciplinary AI minor in spring 2027, according to [GeekWire](https://www.geekwire.com/2026/not-just-for-coders-uws-upcoming-ai-minor-will-reach-beyond-the-computer-science-school/). The minor would require students to take classes covering AI ethics and technical foundations, complete an AI project, and select from various AI electives. *GeekWire* noted the program is co-led by an anthropologist and a computer scientist, signaling the minor’s reach beyond traditional tech students. The engineering school currently offers an AI and machine learning certificate at the master’s level, along with several other related AI [programs](https://www.ai.uw.edu/student-success/ai-degree-programs/), but the new minor would open the door to students across more departments. “Allen School researchers are at the forefront of exciting developments in AI spanning machine learning, computer vision, natural language processing, robotics, and more,” the Paul G. Allen School’s [website](https://www.cs.washington.edu/research/artificial-intelligence/) stated. UW isn’t the only university rushing to add AI programs. [*The New York Times*](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/06/08/us/ai-college-degrees.html) reported there are at least 74 AI majors and 89 minors offered throughout the U.S. ## AI expansion met with criticism in Seattle But while UW is joining those rising to meet artificial intelligence, critics in Seattle worry about the strain new data centers could have on the environment and electricity bills. Four companies [originally approached](https://mynorthwest.com/technology/seattle-ai-data-center/4228840) Seattle City Light in April about building five new data centers, which would have roughly consumed one-third of the city’s average daily electricity use. In response, the Seattle City Council placed a [temporary freeze](https://council.seattle.gov/2026/06/09/city-council-passes-emergency-data-center-moratorium-and-policy-framework/) on new data center siting while the city conducts impact studies. “The passing of today’s moratorium does not stop AI or data centers. It does, however, pause to hold those accountable in the development of data centers, while understanding the long-lasting impact on people and land,” Seattle City Council member Debora Juarez stated. Seattle currently hosts about 30 data centers, most of which are relatively small. *Contributing: Luke Duecy, KIRO Newsradio* *This story was originally published on* [*mynorthwest.com.*](https://mynorthwest.com/technology/ai-minor-uw/4249651 "https://mynorthwest.com/technology/ai-minor-uw/4249651") * News * [Local](/news/local) * [Stream Now](/video) * [National/World](/news/nation-world/) * [Live Traffic](/traffic) * PinPoint Weather * [Stormtracker HD](/weather/dopplerstream) * About Us * [Contact Us](/station/contact-us/) * [What's on KIRO 7](/station/tvlistings) * [KIRO 7 Cares](https://www.kiro7.com/cares/) * [KIRO 7 Public File](https://publicfiles.fcc.gov/tv-profile/kiro-tv) * [KIRO 7 Public File Contact](/station/kiro-7-public-file-contact/7AO75MKEBVELNHMXOJBR3BFHEE/) * [KIRO 7 Closed Captioning Contact](/station/kiro-7-closed-captioning-contact/GQMASLGE4FEGLMCAI2JQLKYA3I/) * [FCC Applications](/broadcast/fcc-applications/MEOQ7ZZUMVEXNB5U756GAHJZCE/) * [Jobs at KIRO 7](https://careers.cmg.com/) © 2026 [Cox Media Group](https://www.coxmediagroup.com).
